run.output: Query executed successfully, returned 4 rows in 55ms

validation.comparison_result: All 4 result rows match ground truth. Floating-point precision differences (<0.1%) are acceptable for aggregation arithmetic.

profiling.notes: perf tool not available; timing captured via std::chrono. Hardware: 64-core Xeon Gold 5218, AVX-512, 44MB L3 cache, HDD storage

Q1.notes: Semantic bug from iter 1 fixed (cutoff_date=10472 correct). Scanned 59,142,609 rows. 63.6% of DuckDB best time (86.44ms), 270x faster than PostgreSQL.

Q1.root_cause: Query is well-parallelized across 64 cores with morsel-driven execution. Execution consists of: (1) 59.1M row scan with date filter (~20ms estimated), (2) local aggregation in 64 threads (~25ms estimated), (3) output formatting & merge (~10ms). The tight loop in lines 168-204 performs filtering and aggregation without significant stalls. Main opportunities: SIMD vectorization for floating-point calculations (disc_price, charge), L3 cache tuning for morsel size, and prefetching for HDD I/O latency hiding.

analysis.overall: Query 1 (Pricing Summary Report) PASSED correctness validation and achieved sub-100ms execution time (55ms) on a 60M-row lineitem table. The semantic bug from iteration 1 is fixed: cutoff_date=10472 correctly filters to 1998-09-02. The parallel architecture (64 threads, morsel-driven 100K-row chunks) is well-designed and scales near-linearly. Current performance is competitive with DuckDB best-case (86.44ms) but slower than its average (108.83ms). This suggests the implementation is close to optimal for a straightforward scan-filter-aggregate; further gains require algorithmic innovation (SIMD, cache-aware tuning, HDD prefetching) rather than parallelism.

performance_optimizations.description: Lines 175-182 compute disc_price = price * (1 - disc) and charge = disc_price * (1 + tax) for each row sequentially. Current: scalar, 1 row per iteration. Target: Use AVX-512 (available per lscpu Flags) to process 8 doubles in parallel. Load 8 values from extendedprice, discount, tax into __m512d registers, perform horizontal multiplications: disc_price_vec = _mm512_mul_pd(price_vec, _mm512_sub_pd(one, disc_vec)); charge_vec = _mm512_mul_pd(disc_price_vec, _mm512_add_pd(one, tax_vec)). Store results back. Integrate with horizontal aggregation: after computing 8 rows' disc_price/charge, directly accumulate into sum_disc_price and sum_charge registers before scalar merge. Expected speedup: 6-8x on arithmetic operations (from ~15ms to ~2-3ms), total wall-clock 55ms → ~48-50ms.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 8-12% wall-clock improvement (3-7ms reduction). The tight arithmetic loop (lines 180-182) is CPU-bound and well-suited to SIMD. Floating-point multiply-add throughput should improve from 1-2 per cycle to 6-8 per cycle per core.

performance_optimizations.technique: Selective Column Loading with Zone Maps for l_shipdate
performance_optimizations.description: Currently loads full l_shipdate.bin (60M rows × 4 bytes = 240MB) via mmap. Per storage_design.json, lineitem is sorted by l_shipdate with block_size=100K rows. Implement zone maps: precompute min/max shipdate for each 100K-row block, store in separate binary file (600 blocks × 2 × 4 bytes = 4.8KB). During scan initialization, binary search in zone maps to identify blocks where max(shipdate) >= 10472 (cutoff date). Skip blocks entirely where max(shipdate) < 10472. Estimated: ~94% of rows pass filter (per workload_analysis.json selectivity 0.941), so minimal block skipping possible (~6% of blocks). However, zone map enables future queries with tighter date ranges. On HDD, prefetch only needed blocks and avoid touching pages for blocks with max_shipdate < cutoff (saves ~15MB random I/O). Implementation: read zone map file into memory (~5KB), iterate 600 entries, mark skip_block flags, pass to scan loop.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 3-8% wall-clock improvement (2-4ms reduction). Primary benefit is reduced page cache pollution and HDD seeks on blocks marked for skipping. Secondary benefit: enables prefetching of only needed pages, reducing latency stalls. Current HDD latency ~10ms per seek; skipping 5-6% of blocks eliminates ~1 seek.

performance_optimizations.technique: Thread Tuning: Reduce Morsel Size for L3 Cache Efficiency
performance_optimizations.description: Current morsel_size = 100K rows (line 157). With 64 threads, each thread has ~44MB L3 / 32 = ~1.4MB allocation (shared L3 per core pair on Xeon Gold). A morsel of 100K rows × 7 columns × 8 bytes ≈ 5.6MB working set, exceeding L3 and causing DRAM traffic. Reduce morsel_size to 25K rows (~1.4MB per morsel). This keeps hot data in L3, reduces memory bandwidth stalls, and improves cache hit rates. Trade-off: more thread synchronization overhead (mutex lock on line 209 invoked 4x more often), but expected benefit outweighs sync cost because L3 hits are ~4 cycles vs. DRAM at ~200 cycles. Alternative: use lock-free atomic aggregation for reduce contention, but 64 threads competing on 6 groups suggests low contention (handled efficiently by current linear search in local_entries).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% wall-clock improvement (3-5ms reduction). Estimated from: 55ms × (DRAM_cost / avg_memory_cost). If current cache hit rate is ~40% (7 columns × 100K rows = 5.6MB > 1.4MB L3), reducing to 25K rows should achieve ~70% hit rate, saving ~0.4-0.6ms per scan. Over 64 threads in parallel, amortizes to ~3-5ms total.

performance_optimizations.technique: Prefetching Strategy for HDD I/O Latency Hiding
performance_optimizations.description: HDD random seek latency ~10ms; sequential read throughput ~100-150 MB/s. Current mmap-based load relies on OS page cache, which may not prefetch optimally for multi-threaded access patterns. Implement explicit prefetching: before entering main scan loop (line 165), use __builtin_prefetch() to prefetch 64 bytes ahead within each column. Within hot loop (lines 168-204), prefetch next 3 morsels of l_shipdate (sparse temporal locality). Specifically: at end of iteration i, prefetch shipdate[i + morsel_size + offset] for offset in [0, 3*morsel_size]. This masks HDD latency by overlapping prefetch with current computation. Alternative: use madvise(MADV_SEQUENTIAL) on mmapped regions (requires system call but signals OS to aggregate sequential I/O).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5% wall-clock improvement (1-3ms reduction). Prefetching in software is effective when HDD latency is critical path. With 64 threads saturating HDD bandwidth, prefetch misses are likely. Hiding 10ms HDD latency behind computation could save 1-2ms per thread, aggregating to ~2-3ms observed improvement.

performance_optimizations.description: Line 170 has a conditional branch: if (shipdate[idx] <= cutoff_date). With 94% selectivity, branch predictor is correct 94% of time, but still incurs occasional mispredictions on date boundaries. Convert to branchless filtering: use arithmetic tricks to avoid branch. Example: mask = (shipdate[idx] <= cutoff_date) ? 1 : 0; if (mask) { /* aggregate */ }. Compiler often recognizes this pattern and generates cmov instead of branch. Alternatively: use lookup table for date classification (impossible here) or compute cumulative contributions and subtract. For Q1, direct approach is unlikely to yield significant gains (branch prediction is already 94% accurate), but demonstrates technique for future queries with more irregular predicates.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0-2% wall-clock improvement (0-1ms reduction). Branch prediction is already highly accurate (94% selectivity), so misprediction penalty is minimal. Benefit only materializes if query expands to include other predicates with lower predictability.

summary: Query 1 PASSED correctness validation with 55ms execution time. The critical semantic bug from iteration 1 (cutoff_date off by 1 day) is fixed. Performance is excellent: 55ms for 60M-row scan is 63.6% of DuckDB's best time (86.44ms) and 270x faster than PostgreSQL (15023ms). The parallel architecture (64 threads, morsel-driven execution, 6-group aggregation) is well-designed. Remaining optimization opportunities are incremental: (1) SIMD vectorization of floating-point arithmetic (8-12% gain, medium risk), (2) Zone maps for l_shipdate to skip blocks (3-8% gain, low risk), (3) L3 cache tuning via morsel size reduction (5-10% gain, medium risk), (4) HDD prefetching (2-5% gain, low risk). Recommend pursuing SIMD first (highest impact and well-scoped to lines 175-182), then L3 tuning. Zone maps and prefetching are safe low-risk options. After these optimizations, query may approach DuckDB minimum (86.44ms) or better if vectorization unlocks SIMD parallelism on 64 cores (potential 3-5x arithmetic speedup = 48-50ms total).
